Wait, but in C++, the vector is a member variable. So, in the starter code, the class has a constructor and the book function.

So, the code would be:

class MyCalendarThree {
public:
    MyCalendarThree() : current_max(0) {}
    
    int book(int startTime, int endTime) {
        events.push_back({startTime, endTime});
        int count = 0;
        for (const auto& event : events) {
            if (startTime < event.second && endTime > event.first) {
                count++;
            }
        }
        current_max = max(current_max, count);
        return current_max;
    }
private:
    vector<pair<int, int>> events;
    int current_max;
};

solution:
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

using namespace std;

class MyCalendarThree {
public:
    MyCalendarThree() : current_max(0) {
        
    }
    
    int book(int startTime, int endTime) {
        events.push_back(make_pair(startTime, endTime));
        int count = 0;
        for (const auto& event : events) {
            if (startTime < event.second && endTime > event.first) {
                count++;
            }
        }
        current_max = max(current_max, count);
        return current_max;
    }
private:
    vector<pair<int, int>> events;
    int current_max;
};
```

efficiency: HIGH

time complexity: O(n^2)

space complexity: O(n)

energy implications: LOW

explanation: The solution efficiently tracks each event and calculates the maximum k-booking by checking overlaps with all previous events. It ensures optimal performance given the constraints.
